_comment: HAND-AUTHORED adjudications, merged over the extracted hypothesis by scripts/build-capability-manifest.mjs. This file is the source of truth and is never generated; capabilities.json IS generated and must never be hand-edited. Each entry records what a human decided about one (op|enum, dialect) cell: `kind` native|absent, `name` the engine's OWN term for it, and `refusal` the exact message when absent. Filling all four dialects with the SAME name is what makes a capability shared — sharedness is DERIVED from the names agreeing, never authored. Leave `name` empty when the kind is settled but the surface term is still a design decision; the cell then stays unadjudicated on purpose.

top._why: T-SQL has TOP and, since 2012, OFFSET/FETCH. They are two different constructs, not two spellings of one — SQL Server gained OFFSET/FETCH later for standards compliance and kept TOP. So MSSQL genuinely has both, and the other three have only the LIMIT concept, which is reached by limit(). Through 10.x .top(n) was SILENTLY DISCARDED on the other three: the row cap the caller wrote vanished with no error and no clause. Fixed 2026-07-20.

mysql.refusal: MySQL has no TOP clause — use limit() instead

postgres.refusal: Postgres has no TOP clause — use limit() instead

sqlite.refusal: SQLite has no TOP clause — use limit() instead

limitWithTies._why: WITH TIES — return the extra rows that tie with the last one under the ORDER BY. Genuinely native on both engines that have it, and NOT a synthesis on either: T-SQL spells it `SELECT TOP (n) WITH TIES`, which is part of TOP's own grammar, and Postgres 13+ spells it `FETCH FIRST n ROWS WITH TIES`. Different keywords, one capability, so the shared name stands. MySQL and SQLite have no equivalent at all — before the sweep MSSQL emitted an unparseable `OFFSET/FETCH ... WITH TIES` (ac9aeb4), which is why this looked like an emulation and was withheld from extraction.

mysql.refusal: Limit: MySQL does not support WITH TIES

sqlite.refusal: Limit: SQLite does not support WITH TIES

joinOuterApply._why: Sandy's ruling 2026-07-22: engine-native names per dialect. T-SQL calls it APPLY; Postgres and MySQL call it LATERAL, so each view shows only its own term. Measured, and these are THREE different joins rather than two vocabularies for one — joinCrossApply emits CROSS JOIN LATERAL, joinOuterApply emits LEFT JOIN LATERAL ... ON TRUE, and the pre-existing joinLateral emits JOIN LATERAL ... ON <cond>. So joinCrossLateral/joinLeftLateral are surface aliases over the first two, and joinLateral is untouched; collapsing any of them would delete a capability rather than rename one. SQLite has neither vocabulary.

sqlite.refusal: Join: SQLite does not support LATERAL joins

forUpdate._why:
Postgres and MySQL have a statement-level FOR UPDATE that locks every table the statement reads — one clause, no placement question. MSSQL has no such clause at all: its nearest equivalent is a WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK) hint, and T-SQL's table_hint production attaches to a table_or_view_name, i.e. ONE table reference. Through 10.x the hint was emitted only in the FROM-table branch, so a joined table was read at plain READ COMMITTED while the caller believed the whole result was locked, and a derived-table or TVF source got no hint anywhere. Silent, and a data-integrity bug rather than a syntax one.

The corpus could not have caught it: of 322 cases, 8 use a row lock and NONE combines one with a join or a non-table FROM. A hand-written test asserted the bug outright, named 'MSSQL applies the hint to the FROM table even alongside a JOIN'.

Fixed 2026-07-20: the hint now reaches every table reference, and MSSQL REFUSES where T-SQL has nowhere to put one. SQLite has no row-level locking at any version — its locking is whole-database/WAL-level.

MSSQL NAME = 'updlock' (Sandy's decision 2026-07-20). forUpdate is Postgres/MySQL vocabulary; a T-SQL DBA reaches for UPDLOCK, which is exactly what the hint emits — WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK). So the shared portable name does NOT derive here: Postgres and MySQL name it forUpdate, MSSQL names it updlock, and those are different real terms. The METHOD rename to .updlock() on the MSSQL builder lands with the typed surface; this cell records the target name now so the surface work has it.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

onConflictDoUpdate._why:
Postgres (9.5+) and SQLite (3.24+) have a real conflict TARGET: ON CONFLICT (cols) DO UPDATE. MySQL does not — ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE fires on ANY unique or primary key and its grammar accepts no column list. Through 10.x naming columns on MySQL was silently discarded, so 'conflict on email' became 'conflict on any unique key', which matches a DIFFERENT set of rows and can update rows the caller never intended. Fixed 2026-07-20: MySQL refuses a named target and still accepts the untargeted form, which is honest about what the engine means. MSSQL is NOT adjudicated here — it has no upsert primitive at all and is currently synthesized as MERGE without HOLDLOCK; that is queued for re-homing under an explicit merge surface.

MSSQL ADJUDICATED 2026-07-20: T-SQL has NO upsert primitive. This used to abandon the INSERT grammar and synthesize a MERGE — a different statement with different atomicity, trigger and error semantics, which the caller never wrote and could not see. It was unsafe as written too: an un-hinted MERGE used as an upsert is race-prone at READ COMMITTED and can raise a duplicate-key violation, which ON CONFLICT never does. The repo already knew this (forShare emitted WITH (HOLDLOCK)), so the omission was an oversight. MERGE is genuine native T-SQL and should return as an EXPLICIT surface named in T-SQL's own vocabulary — that belongs with the per-engine typed builders, because naming it now would mint `mergeMssql()`, the suffixed form Sandy rejected.

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no upsert — T-SQL expresses this with MERGE, which is a different statement with different concurrency semantics; write it explicitly

onConflictDoNothing._why:
Same conflict-target story as onConflictDoUpdate. Postgres/SQLite spell it DO NOTHING with an optional target; MySQL spells it INSERT IGNORE, which is a genuinely different construct — it suppresses OTHER errors too, not just duplicate keys — and accepts no target. Both engines' terms are real, and they are DIFFERENT terms, so this does not derive shared. MSSQL unadjudicated pending the merge re-home.

forShare._why:
Postgres and MySQL 8.0+ have a statement-level FOR SHARE. MSSQL does NOT, and the WITH (HOLDLOCK, ROWLOCK) it used to emit was not a near-equivalent: HOLDLOCK is a SYNONYM FOR SERIALIZABLE and takes key-range locks that prevent phantom inserts, which FOR SHARE does not do. Emitting it silently escalated the caller's isolation level. T-SQL's hint taxonomy splits into lock hints (UPDLOCK/XLOCK/ROWLOCK) and isolation hints (HOLDLOCK/REPEATABLEREAD/SERIALIZABLE), and there is no shared-mode counterpart to UPDLOCK in the first group.

REPEATABLEREAD was considered and rejected: it is still a read-isolation instruction that happens to leave shared locks behind rather than a lock REQUEST, and it changes how every other row the statement scans is read. Under 'no approximations' that is a synthesis.

Refused on MSSQL 2026-07-20. It should return under T-SQL's own vocabulary with the per-engine typed builders, where a caller naming HOLDLOCK or REPEATABLEREAD gets exactly what they asked for. forUpdate is unaffected — UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK is Microsoft's own documented idiom for it. SQLite has no row-level locking at any version.

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no shared row lock — HOLDLOCK is a SERIALIZABLE isolation hint, not FOR SHARE; use forUpdate() or take the isolation level explicitly

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

merge._why: MERGE is native T-SQL and exists on no other dialect. RE-HOMED 2026-07-20 as an explicit surface — QueryBuilder.merge((m) => …) over a MergeBuilder — after Sandy approved bringing it back. It expresses MERGE as T-SQL defines it (INTO/USING/ON/WHEN MATCHED/WHEN NOT MATCHED [BY SOURCE]/OUTPUT/mandatory semicolon/WITH (HOLDLOCK)), NOT the merge(conflictColumns, updates) upsert-keyhole a reviewer flagged as a worse lie than mergeMssql(). Through 7015f6c it had been REFUSED, because the only MERGE in the codebase was the silent INSERT-becomes-MERGE upsert substitution, which was removed. This is its honest return. HOLDLOCK is exposed via .holdlock(), documented as the concurrency-safe form, and NOT injected — the builder emits the MERGE the caller wrote. Name = 'merge' with no suffix: it collides with nothing, so when the per-engine typed builders land it moves verbatim to MssqlQueryBuilder and the runtime refusal on the other three becomes a compile-time absence.

mysql.refusal: MySQL has no MERGE statement — it is native T-SQL only

postgres.refusal: Postgres has no MERGE statement — it is native T-SQL only

sqlite.refusal: SQLite has no MERGE statement — it is native T-SQL only

into._why: MERGE is native T-SQL and exists nowhere else here, so every part of its grammar is MSSQL-only — not just the merge() entry point but each sub-builder method the MergeBuilder exposes (builder/merge.ts). The other three refuse with 'Merge: <Dialect> has no MERGE statement — it is native T-SQL only', verified by running each one. Recorded because the manifest was shipping these as native + adjudicated on all four: presence on a typed view is not performance, and these methods live on a sub-builder the view never gated. SQLEasy 10 synthesized a MERGE on MSSQL for upsert and it was race-prone without HOLDLOCK, which is exactly why holdlock() is part of this grammar rather than implied.

fromTableWithOwner._why: Owner (schema) qualification in the FROM clause. Sandy's ruling 2026-07-22: KEEP the WithOwner name rather than renaming the five-op family to WithSchema — it is what SQLEasy has always used, it is consistent across the family, and a vocabulary preference does not justify five breaking renames. All three supporting dialects therefore share one name, so sharedness derives. MySQL is absent because its 'schema' IS the database the connection already targets: there is no second namespace to qualify with, and the parser refuses a NON-EMPTY owner while leaving the method callable in its degenerate form.

mysql.refusal: From: MySQL does not support table owners

joinTableWithOwner._why: Owner (schema) qualification. In MySQL the 'schema' IS the database the connection already targets, so there is no second namespace to qualify with and the parser refuses a NON-EMPTY owner: '<Area>: MySQL does not support table owners'. The method deliberately stays ON the MySQL view (typed-views.ts) because an EMPTY owner is legal there — a conditional refusal, not a hidden method. That is precisely what made the manifest wrong: presence was being read as native, so it asserted MySQL performs owner qualification while the parser threw. The CAPABILITY is absent on MySQL; the method merely remains callable in its degenerate form.

forUpdateNowait._why:
Postgres and MySQL carry the wait mode on the statement-level locking clause: default-row-lock.ts:32-38 appends ' NOWAIT' after 'FOR UPDATE'. One clause, no placement question. MSSQL has no such clause, so mssqlRowLockHint (default-row-lock.ts:99-102) returns ' WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK, NOWAIT)' and default-from.ts:63-66 / default-join.ts:133-136 stamp it onto EVERY table reference. That is native, not a synthesis — NOWAIT is a first-class T-SQL table hint — but it is native under MSSQL's OWN name: typed-views.ts:108 hides `forUpdateNowait` from the MSSQL view as an explicit engine-native rename (comment at :105-106) and typed-views.ts:149 gives MSSQL `updlockNowait` instead, which the other three views drop. Both halves of that name are genuine T-SQL terms (UPDLOCK is a lock hint, NOWAIT is a table hint), which is why it is adjudicated here and `updlockSkipLocked` is not. Sharedness derives across postgres+mysql only; MSSQL's name genuinely differs, exactly as forUpdate/updlock already records.

TWO TRAPS. The MSSQL hint is TABLE-scoped, so any source T-SQL cannot hint — derived table, TVF, LATERAL body, raw fragment — is refused by refuseUnplaceableMssqlRowLock (default-row-lock.ts:61-74, called from default-from.ts:31/76/96/125 and default-join.ts:34/148) with a SECOND, different message: 'MSSQL cannot lock <source> — a locking hint attaches to a table reference only'. And T-SQL's NOWAIT is effectively LOCK_TIMEOUT 0 for that table and aborts (error 1222) on any ungrantable lock, where the Postgres/MySQL NOWAIT fails only on the target rows — same term, wider blast radius. MySQL emits unconditionally with no version gate (Dialect carries no version field), which hard-floors the library at MySQL 8.0.1+; stated in prose at comparison-operator.ts:153-156, not enforced.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

forUpdateSkipLocked._why: Postgres and MySQL append ' SKIP LOCKED' to the statement-level locking clause. MSSQL reaches the same behaviour with the READPAST table hint (WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK, READPAST)) — native, and Microsoft's own documented queue-table idiom, not a synthesis. Sandy's ruling 2026-07-22: MSSQL's surface name is updlockReadpast, T-SQL end to end. It was updlockSkipLocked, which was half-translated and contradicted the already-adjudicated RowLockWait.SkipLocked cell recording MSSQL's own term as READPAST.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

forShareNowait._why:
Postgres and MySQL 8.0+ have a statement-level FOR SHARE and the wait modifier rides on it identically to the exclusive form: default-row-lock.ts:33 picks 'FOR SHARE' and :35-36 appends ' NOWAIT'. MSSQL is absent for a reason that has NOTHING to do with the wait mode: mssqlRowLockHint checks RowLockMode.ForShare FIRST (default-row-lock.ts:90-96) and throws before it ever reaches the wait branch at :99, so no wait mode makes forShare reachable on T-SQL. The taxonomy has no shared-mode counterpart to UPDLOCK, and HOLDLOCK is a SERIALIZABLE synonym that silently escalated the caller's isolation level. Consistent with the settled forShare cell.

MySQL is the version-sensitive cell: FOR SHARE, NOWAIT and SKIP LOCKED are all emitted UNCONDITIONALLY (Dialect in configuration/configuration.ts has no version field, so there is no gate to fail), hard-flooring the library at MySQL 8.0.1+ — pre-8.0 has only LOCK IN SHARE MODE, which admits no NOWAIT at all.

CROSS-CHECK NIT, worth fixing when the typed surface lands: the MSSQL refusal tells the caller to 'use forUpdate()', but `forUpdate` is precisely what the MSSQL view hides (typed-views.ts:107) in favour of `updlock()`. The message names a method an MSSQL user cannot call. Left verbatim here because the manifest records the message that ships.

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no shared row lock — HOLDLOCK is a SERIALIZABLE isolation hint, not FOR SHARE; use forUpdate() or take the isolation level explicitly

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

forShareSkipLocked._why:
Identical shape to forShareNowait: default-row-lock.ts:33 emits 'FOR SHARE' and :37-39 appends ' SKIP LOCKED' for Postgres and MySQL. MSSQL is absent, and the ORDER of the guard is the load-bearing detail — mssqlRowLockHint rejects RowLockMode.ForShare at default-row-lock.ts:90-96 BEFORE it ever consults rowLock.wait at :107, so READPAST (which MSSQL genuinely has, and which the forUpdate family uses) is never reached. There is no shared-mode lock hint for it to attach to. That is the trap for this whole group: the absence is about lock strength, not wait mode.

MySQL carries the same unconditional 8.0.1+ floor as forShareNowait. SQLite throws at default-row-lock.ts:21-26, the first statement of emitTrailingRowLockClause, before mode or wait is inspected — no argument escapes it. Both MSSQL and SQLite absences are double-floored: typed-views.ts:92 and :203 hide the method, and the parser throws.

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no shared row lock — HOLDLOCK is a SERIALIZABLE isolation hint, not FOR SHARE; use forUpdate() or take the isolation level explicitly

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

frame._why:
default-window.ts:104-137 is ONE shared emitter — 'ROWS '/'RANGE ' followed by either 'BETWEEN start AND end' or the SQL-standard single-bound shorthand — with exactly one dialect branch: :114-124 throws on MSSQL when FrameUnit.Range is paired with a numeric-offset bound, because T-SQL's RANGE admits only UNBOUNDED PRECEDING, CURRENT ROW and UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING while Postgres, MySQL 8.0+ and SQLite 3.28+ all take `RANGE 5 PRECEDING`. That refusal is conditional on an ARGUMENT rather than on the method, so `frame` rightly appears in none of the AbsentOn sets — typed-views.ts:66-75 is explicit that a method refusing only in a certain combination stays shared and runtime-guarded. All four names agree, so sharedness derives.

TWO TRAPS. `frame` is a WindowBuilder method, not a QueryBuilder key, and typed-views.ts:36 deliberately leaves WindowBuilder callbacks unrebound — so nothing about the frame surface is enforced at compile time on any dialect and the MSSQL guard is runtime-only. And `frameRaw` bypasses that guard entirely (default-window.ts:109-111 emits frame.raw and returns before the branch), so `frameRaw('RANGE 5 PRECEDING')` still ships invalid T-SQL.

STILL OPEN, and NOT closed by this cell: the MSSQL RANGE+offset refusal is recorded NOWHERE in the manifest. FrameUnit.Range is adjudicated native on all four dialects with no note, and there is no bound-level cell for a numeric offset — so a manifest built from these cells will not surface the one real MSSQL frame constraint. GROUPS and EXCLUDE are also missing entirely: enums/frame-unit.ts declares only Rows and Range, and neither token appears anywhere in ts/query/src. Both are real on Postgres 11+ and SQLite 3.28+ and absent on MySQL/MSSQL — capabilities nobody wrote, not absent cells, so they belong on the missing-capability track.

groupByRollup._why:
ROLLUP is genuinely native on three engines and absent from SQLite, but MySQL's spelling diverges: default-group-by.ts:94-98 branches on DatabaseType.Mysql and emits the column list followed by the trailing modifier ' WITH ROLLUP', where MSSQL and Postgres take the standard grouping-element form at :100-102, 'ROLLUP (...)'. That is NOT an emulation — WITH ROLLUP is MySQL's own construct under the same word, nothing is synthesized out of other constructs, and both spellings produce identical super-aggregate rows because SQLEasy can only ever roll up the WHOLE grouping list. Precedent controls the naming: limitWithTies already rules that a shared phrase spelled through different grammar (`TOP … WITH TIES` vs `FETCH FIRST … WITH TIES`) is still one real term, so ROLLUP derives shared across mssql/mysql/postgres rather than splitting into a MySQL-only `withRollup`. SQLite is double-floored: AbsentOnSqlite at typed-views.ts:209 plus the throw at default-group-by.ts:90-92.

LIVE FINDING, argument axis, NOT closed by this cell: because 'WITH ROLLUP' is a whole-clause modifier while 'ROLLUP(...)' is one grouping ELEMENT, the mixed form `GROUP BY a, ROLLUP(b)` — legal on MSSQL/Postgres, impossible on MySQL — is inexpressible, and the parser does not refuse it. default-group-by.ts:51 skips the plain-column loop once a modifier is present and :81-84 prefers the explicit column list over plainColumns, so `.groupByColumn('o','region').groupByRollup([product])` SILENTLY DROPS `region` on all three dialects. No corpus case covers it. Same family as the `top()` silent-discard, on the argument axis.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite has no ROLLUP — use groupByRaw

groupByCube._why:
CUBE is native on MSSQL and Postgres and genuinely absent on both MySQL and SQLite — the only op in this group where the two refusing dialects land together, for unrelated reasons (MySQL has ROLLUP but never gained CUBE; SQLite has no grouping extensions at all). default-group-by.ts:127-129 emits 'CUBE (' + column list + ')' for the two engines that have it. Nothing is synthesized on the refusing side — no UNION ALL over grouping levels, no CASE-built grouping key — which is the check that matters most here, since a CUBE is the easiest of the three to fake. Both refusals are double-floored: AbsentOnMysql typed-views.ts:134 + throw at :120-125, AbsentOnSqlite typed-views.ts:210 + throw at :116-118.

ONE WART, recorded not fixed: MySQL's refusal text points the caller at 'groupByRollup/groupByGroupingSets', but GROUPING SETS is equally absent on MySQL (typed-views.ts:135), so only the rollup and raw halves of that suggestion are actionable. Also inherited from the shared modifier path: as with groupByRollup, a preceding groupByColumn is silently dropped when groupByCube carries an explicit list.

With the injection removed the unqualified call resolves through search_path / the default schema and the cell is simply native.

updateTable._why:
Shared on all four — `updateTable` in every cell, so sharedness derives. builder/query.ts:1692-1705 sets QueryType.Update, pushes a FromState carrying config.defaultOwner, and records mutationTargetIndex; default-update.ts then emits one ordinary UPDATE per dialect.

MSSQL's shape genuinely differs — `UPDATE [u] SET … FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u]` instead of `UPDATE tbl AS alias SET …` (the mssqlAliased branch, default-update.ts:48-59 and 94-100) — and this is NOT an emulation: `UPDATE <table> AS <alias>` is not a T-SQL production at all, so the alias-plus-trailing-FROM form is the engine's own and only spelling of an aliased update target. One native UPDATE statement, no substituted construct. default-update.ts:56-59 also shows the shape is chosen only when an alias is present or joins exist, so an unaliased MSSQL update emits no FROM at all.

TRAP: MySQL only escapes the owner refusal at default-update.ts:39-41 because defaultOwner is '' (dialects/mysql/configuration.ts:14). A caller who overrides defaultOwner on MySQL makes plain `updateTable` throw 'MySQL does not support table owners' with no owner ever passed.

AUDIT-G1 IS NOT RESOLVED BY THIS CELL, and the flag must survive the removal of the forced note. G1 ('SQLite UPDATE … FROM rejected outright, but SQLite 3.33+ supports it') is default-mutation-join.ts:31-44, which returns early when state.joinStates.length === 0 — so it belongs to the joinTable-in-mutation cell, not here; `updateTable` alone is clean on SQLite. It is a MISSING-capability finding, not a dishonest one. Adjacent and still open: Postgres's join-backed UPDATE translates ON conditions into a WHERE predicate (default-mutation-join.ts:45-66) and honestly REFUSES OUTER joins because WHERE cannot express them — guarded, and again on the join cell.

orderByColumn._why:
Plainly shared. emitOrderByTerm (default-order-by.ts:30-66) reads config.databaseType exactly ONCE — line 44, to compute hasNativeNulls — and nothing else in the function branches on dialect: the column is quoted through the dialect's own delimiters and ' ASC'/' DESC' are appended as literals (:56-59), with OrderByDirection.None omitting the token entirely (:61). All four cells emit real, identical-shaped SQL, so the name is `orderByColumn` four times and sharedness derives.

THE DIVERGENCE IS ON THE ENUM AXIS, NOT THIS OP, and it is already adjudicated — do not double-count it. NullsOrder.First/.Last are absent on MSSQL and MySQL (:46-52 throw) and native on Postgres/SQLite (:64), and every MySQL/Postgres throw counted against this op in capabilities.json traces to a NullsOrder case. The sweep's forced note ('NullsOrder First/Last synthesized as CASE WHEN on MySQL and MSSQL') describes behaviour that has been REMOVED: the CASE WHEN synthesis is gone and replaced by a refusal.

THE REAL TRAP IS A SILENT DROP, not an emulation, and it is NOT closed by this cell: defaultOrderBy is only reached on the SELECT path (to-sql.ts:270), because the Insert/Update/Delete branches return earlier — so `orderByColumn` on an UPDATE builder produces no clause and no error on any dialect, and MySQL genuinely has `UPDATE … ORDER BY … LIMIT`. Same shape as the top() silent no-op. The corpus AGREES WITH THAT SILENCE: the case 'update with an owner on mysql throws…' notes that orderByColumn and limit 'still produce nothing — no error, no SQL'. That is the golden-asserts-the-bug pattern.

Two corpus case names are stale relative to the code — 'order by direction none renders desc' and 'order by direction None renders DESC' both PIN `ORDER BY [u].[id],` with no direction token, matching :61. Read the pinned SQL, not the title.

whereJsonContains._why:
The cleanest divide in the JSON group: two engines have a real containment primitive and two have none at all, and the parser REFUSES rather than synthesizing one. default-json.ts:106-110 emits Postgres `col @> $n::jsonb` (the ::jsonb cast added at default-json-predicate.ts:90-94 because the bound parameter is otherwise untyped — a typing necessity for a placeholder, not a rewrite), :112-117 emits MySQL `JSON_CONTAINS(col, ?)` with the closing paren supplied at default-json-predicate.ts:96-98, while :119-124 throws for MSSQL and :126-129 throws for SQLite. Both absences are mirrored structurally in typed-views.ts:95-96 and :212-213 and in the Dart view manifest, so the method is not merely refused but OFF those two views entirely.

NAMES: `whereJsonContains` on both native cells, not the glyphs. Across the shipped manifest every named OP cell is a plain method identifier (updlock, insertIgnore, onConflict, top, merge) and SQL spellings appear ONLY on ENUM cells. The op name becomes the method on the typed view, and '@>' is not a legal method name in any of the five target languages. Recording '@>' and 'JSON_CONTAINS' would also forge a divergence between the only two dialects that HAVE the capability, over one concept both genuinely perform — and sharedness is derived from names agreeing, never authored.

TWO CAUTIONS, neither changing a cell. The SQLite throw at :126-129 is an UNGUARDED fallthrough rather than a DatabaseType.Sqlite test, so DatabaseType.Unknown is also told 'SQLite does not support JSON containment'. And the ONLY corpus case for this op, 'json contains where mysql', binds NULL rather than a document: it carries value {t:'json',…}, but 'json' is not a member of the InputValue union (contract/schema/corpus.ts:25-32) and the conformance driver's toValue has no case for it, so the frozen params read [{t:'null'}] and the raw goldens read `JSON_CONTAINS(\`u\`.\`meta\`, )` and `@> ::jsonb` — neither syntactically valid. The two NATIVE cells here rest on the emitter and on tests/shared/tier3.test.ts:25-29, NOT on that case's value path, which currently attests nothing. The two ABSENT cells rest on hard throws the broken value cannot reach.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support JSON containment — use whereJsonExtract or whereRaw

hintUseIndex._why:
MySQL's table-reference grammar is `tbl_name [[AS] alias] [index_hint_list]`, so USE INDEX is a real MySQL keyword and the other three have nothing SQLEasy exposes under this name. default-hint.ts:43 emits the literal ' USE INDEX (' gated on DatabaseType.Mysql; nothing is synthesized anywhere. The three absences are double-floored: typed-views.ts:101 / :164 / :214, plus a runtime throw.

TWO refusal sites exist and only ONE fires: validateHints (default-hint.ts:99-108) runs unconditionally at to-sql.ts:127 BEFORE the query-type dispatch — placed there precisely so a hint on a non-MySQL INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE is refused rather than silently dropped — which makes the near-identical From-area throw inside mysqlIndexHintForTable (default-hint.ts:32-37, no 'use hintRaw' tail) unreachable in practice. The message recorded here is the validateHints text, which is what a caller actually sees.

Audit F3 (hint emitted before the alias, invalid MySQL) is FIXED and the forced note is stale: default-from.ts:56-60 and default-join.ts:124-128 now call mysqlIndexHintForTable AFTER the ` AS <alias>` block and, on a join, above defaultJoinOns. The corpus case 'mysql use index hint' and tier3.test.ts:236-257 pin the full ordering, not mere presence, and the Dart port agrees.

NAME is `hintUseIndex`, the SQLEasy method name — NOT 'useIndex', which exists nowhere in ts/query/src (the only `useIndex` in the tree is the Dart HintKind enum tag). Op cells diverge from the method name only when a real engine-native RENAME exists as a method on the typed view (forUpdate→updlock, onConflictDoNothing→insertIgnore); there is no `useIndex` method, and typed-views.test.ts:83 asserts `mysql.hintUseIndex('u','idx')` compiles. The engine term 'USE INDEX' already lives on the enum cell HintKind.UseIndex/mysql, and the closest sibling settles it: hintMssqlOption/mssql is 'hintMssqlOption', not 'OPTION'.

AUDIT-G2 IS STILL OPEN and is NOT closed by these cells: MSSQL has a real `WITH (INDEX(...))` and SQLite a real `INDEXED BY`, and SQLEasy offers neither. Those are capabilities nobody wrote — the missing-capability track — not this op's cells, which correctly record that MySQL's USE INDEX has no counterpart elsewhere.

SILENT NO-OP STILL LIVE, MySQL only: mysqlIndexHintForTable is called ONLY from default-from.ts:59 and default-join.ts:127, so a hint set on a MySQL UPDATE/DELETE/INSERT is silently dropped — MySQL's `UPDATE table_reference` grammar does accept index_hint_list. The fix that closed this for the other three dialects (by refusing) left open the one dialect that can honour it. Secondary: default-hint.ts:25 matches on `alias ?? tableName`, so passing the table name when the FROM carries an alias also drops the hint silently.

whereMatch._why: Full-text search, and the multi-column form is where the dialects genuinely part. MySQL is the ONLY one that supports it, and only via a single composite FULLTEXT index over exactly those columns. MSSQL and SQLite always refused it. Postgres used to fabricate the document with concat(a,' ',b) — idiomatic Postgres, but the BUILDER inventing a document rather than handing columns to the engine, which made one call mean four things with one of them manufactured. Sandy's ruling 2026-07-22: refuse it on Postgres too. whereMatchRaw remains the explicit escape hatch. Single-column matching is native on all four and is not affected; the per-dialect MODE differences live on the FullTextMode enum axis, already adjudicated.

selectJsonExtract._why: JSON path extraction. Native on all four, under four completely different spellings — Postgres jsonb_path_query_first, MySQL JSON_EXTRACT/JSON_UNQUOTE, MSSQL JSON_VALUE/JSON_QUERY, SQLite json_extract — so the shared SQLEasy name stands and the mode differences live on the JsonExtractMode enum axis. UNADJUDICABLE until 2026-07-22: the path was built with JSON.stringify, which quotes with " and every dialect reads as a DELIMITED IDENTIFIER. Postgres additionally used `->>`, which takes a KEY rather than a JSONPath, so `$.email` matched nothing and the predicate silently returned NULL. Both fixed; the path is now one JSONPath on all four.

JoinType.Right._why: default-join.ts line 52-54 emits 'RIGHT JOIN ' unconditionally — MySQL is NOT excluded (over-attribution trap avoided). tests/mysql/join.test.ts line 53-65 pins the MySQL RIGHT JOIN output.

JoinType.RightOuter._why: default-join.ts line 55-57 emits 'RIGHT OUTER JOIN ' unconditionally, including MySQL. tests/{mysql,postgres,sqlite}/join.test.ts pin RIGHT OUTER JOIN output for each.

JsonExtractMode.Object._why: In ts/query/src/parser/default-json.ts emitJsonExtractExpression, Object mode (the non-Text branch) emits Postgres '->' (line 36), MySQL JSON_EXTRACT(col, path) without the JSON_UNQUOTE wrap (lines 51-55), MSSQL JSON_QUERY(col, path) (lines 60-66), and for SQLite it THROWS (lines 78-82) because json_extract always returns text. Object mode is not exercised in the corpus (only Text is), but the emitter branches are unambiguous and default-json-predicate.ts reuses this same function for the where/having variants.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite json_extract always returns text — use JsonExtractMode.Text or selectJsonRaw

JsonExtractMode.Text._why: In ts/query/src/parser/default-json.ts emitJsonExtractExpression, Text mode emits distinct native terms per dialect: Postgres '->>' (line 36), MySQL wraps as JSON_UNQUOTE(JSON_EXTRACT(col, path)) (lines 43-47), MSSQL JSON_VALUE(col, path) (lines 69-73), SQLite json_extract(col, path) (lines 85-89). Pinned by tests/shared/tier3.test.ts (Postgres ->>' , MSSQL JSON_VALUE) and contract/corpora/emission/corpus.json around line 21740-21906 (all four dialects).

mysql.refusal: MySQL has no NULLS FIRST/LAST — order by a nullability expression explicitly if you need it

Native SQL term is NULLS LAST; the MySQL/MSSQL refusals are the verbatim throw and match the pinned decisions.json strings.

RowLockMode.ForShare._why: default-row-lock.ts:33 emits the trailing 'FOR SHARE' clause for Postgres/MySQL (row_lock.test.ts asserts `... FOR SHARE;`). MSSQL throws in mssqlRowLockHint (lines 92-96) because T-SQL has no shared-mode counterpart to UPDLOCK — HOLDLOCK is a SERIALIZABLE isolation hint, not FOR SHARE. SQLite throws at lines 22-25.

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no shared row lock — HOLDLOCK is a SERIALIZABLE isolation hint, not FOR SHARE; use forUpdate() or take the isolation level explicitly

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

RowLockMode.ForUpdate._why: default-row-lock.ts:33 emits the trailing 'FOR UPDATE' clause for Postgres/MySQL (pinned in row_lock.test.ts). MSSQL takes the mssqlRowLockHint path which returns ' WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK)' (line 99/112, pinned in mssql_row_lock_coverage.test.ts). SQLite hits the guard at lines 22-25 and throws.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

RowLockMode.None._why: Internal sentinel — "no RowLockMode specified"; adds nothing to the SQL, so it parses on every dialect.

RowLockWait.Default._why: The default block-until-available wait behavior. In default-row-lock.ts neither the trailing-clause path nor mssqlRowLockHint appends any suffix for Default (it falls through to the plain 'FOR UPDATE'/'WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK)'), so it emits nothing and never throws on its own. Treated as a sentinel: native '(none)' on all four dialects.

RowLockWait.Nowait._why: default-row-lock.ts:35-36 appends ' NOWAIT' for Postgres/MySQL (row_lock.test.ts asserts `FOR UPDATE NOWAIT`). MSSQL does NOT throw — mssqlRowLockHint line 101-103 emits ' WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK, NOWAIT)', so NOWAIT is native there (the 'MSSQL refuses' hypothesis is over-attribution; there is no wait-mode throw in source). SQLite throws first at lines 22-25 before any wait suffix.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

RowLockWait.SkipLocked._why: default-row-lock.ts:37-38 appends ' SKIP LOCKED' for Postgres/MySQL (row_lock.test.ts). MSSQL does NOT throw — mssqlRowLockHint line 108-109 emits ' WITH (UPDLOCK, ROWLOCK, READPAST)', READPAST being Microsoft's own term for the skip-locked behavior (mssql_row_lock_coverage.test.ts asserts READPAST). SQLite throws at lines 22-25.

sqlite.refusal: SQLite does not support row locking (FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE)

UpsertAction.DoNothing._why: Per ts/query/src/parser/default-upsert.ts and default-insert.ts: MySQL DoNothing takes the isMysqlInsertIgnore() path — defaultInsert emits an 'INSERT IGNORE' prefix (default-insert.ts:59-61) and emitUpsertClause emits nothing (default-upsert.ts:92-95); pinned by tests/shared/upsert.test.ts:50 ('INSERT IGNORE INTO ...'). Postgres/SQLite fall through to the shared branch emitting ' ON CONFLICT' + optional (cols) + ' DO NOTHING' (default-upsert.ts:103-113; upsert.test.ts:14,37). MSSQL is refused up front in defaultInsert (lines 45-51) whenever state.upsertState is present, before emitUpsertClause runs — MERGE is a separate statement/enum, not this INSERT-upsert path (test upsert.test.ts:80 asserts /MSSQL has no upsert/).

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no upsert — T-SQL expresses this with MERGE, which is a different statement with different concurrency semantics; write it explicitly

UpsertAction.DoUpdate._why: Per ts/query/src/parser/default-upsert.ts: MySQL DoUpdate emits ' ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE ' + SET list (default-upsert.ts:97-99; tests/shared/upsert.test.ts:109). Postgres/SQLite emit ' ON CONFLICT' + (conflict cols, required here) + ' DO UPDATE SET ' + SET list (default-upsert.ts:103-120; upsert.test.ts:95). MSSQL is refused in defaultInsert (lines 45-51) whenever an upsertState is present — the emitUpsertClause MSSQL guard (default-upsert.ts:72-77) is unreachable because defaultInsert throws first; MERGE is a separate native T-SQL surface, not this path (test tier3.test.ts:82 asserts /MSSQL has no upsert/).

mssql.refusal: MSSQL has no upsert — T-SQL expresses this with MERGE, which is a different statement with different concurrency semantics; write it explicitly
